Alright, so a very, very warm welcome back to the final lesson of module one. Five C's of a successful conversion coach. You know, let me start off by saying that one of the goals of this program is to really arm you with the tools to accelerate the process of building trust and intimacy with your audience and doing so at scale in a way that makes it easier for you to facilitate or coach the conversion. And this applies to online entrepreneurs speaking to their own audience as well and maybe even especially for copywriters and freelancers who are doing this for their clients. Because there's this myth, you don't need years to develop that relationship and bond. In fact, I want you to take a second right now to think about someone who came into your life, you know, seemingly out of nowhere. And after maybe one exchange, one drink, one fleeting moment, you felt an instant connection. You know, that's what we're trying to achieve here. Now if you're wondering what this has to do with copywriting and online product launches and funnels, the short answer is everything. You know, a sale is a transformation and unless you've earned the right to influence that transformation, no copywriting trick in the world will help you get the conversion. So if you'll stay with me here, I want to do a quick two minute exercise before we dive into the main part of this lesson. I want you to close your eyes and really think about the person you feel most at ease with, most comfortable. So someone you really, really trust. You know, someone who's advice you're most open to taking, you know, most willing to explore and embrace what they have to offer, which is pretty much just another way of saying they're influential to you. And once you have this person in your mind, I want you to take just a few extra minutes, moments, sorry, to tap into why this person is so influential and important to you. Really ask what is it about them that makes you feel so good, so at ease and so ready to transform in their presence? What are those qualities? And if you feel compelled to do so, I fully invite you to take a second to pause this video right now and write down to sketch out anything that comes to mind. Anything that comes to mind about why you feel so at ease with this person, why you are so ready to embrace what they have to say and act on what they have to say. So take just a few seconds to pause this video and write out anything that comes to mind, and then we'll go a little deeper on this in just a few seconds. All right, so hopefully you took a moment to explore that. You know, if this was a live workshop, this is where I'd love to hear what was going through your mind, but for now, I do want to offer some possible insights that may have come up for you and just see how that matches with some of the insights you generated. You know, a lot of the times when I do this exercise in a live workshop, people are like super astonished to realize that the person that came up for them wasn't necessarily a parent, a partner, or a lifelong friend. In fact, almost paradoxically, people resist the influence of the people who they know best, which sounds kind of strange, right? Like why might you be more influenceable by a person who just swiped right on a dating app than the person who's swiped the dirt off your face when you were young? And here's why. Here's my thesis around that. It's that we're influenced not by the ones we know best, but the ones who make the best effort to know us. You know, these are the people who ask us questions and actually listen, actually listen to what we have to say instead of just assuming they know us. They're present with their hopes, our fears, our dreams. And in the best cases, they're present with the underlying or unconscious factors around them. They're the ones who see things that we haven't even seen for ourselves and they can reflect it back to us. So not only do they make the best effort to know us, but sometimes they show that they know us better than ourselves. They can add layers of meaning behind our actions, our inactions, our fears, our dreams, our hopes, our desires. And because we feel they get us on such a deep level, you know, deeper than we may even know ourselves, that's what earns our trust and the ability to inspire and empower our next action. So here's what's at play. And I really want you to start embodying these traits and characteristics in your relationship with your audience. The first principle is contextual awareness. You know, this is a fancy word of saying they understand us. You know, they can reflect back our context and shine a mirror, giving us just greater visibility on our circumstance, both internal and external. And it's with this awareness, this new found gift of awareness that it gives us the ability to commit to new directions. You know, in a practical copywriting sense, clients get frustrated sometimes with me because I can't evaluate how good an email is or isn't without context, because the effectiveness, the strength of an email is completely relative to what's needed in that moment. You know, I need to know where did they come from? You know, what did they see just prior to this? What did they do or what didn't they do? Because sometimes inactions are more telling than actions. You know, so in order to make people feel understood, you need to first prove that you know how they got here, you know, validate their experience, show them that you get everything that they brought to this very moment. You know, in a one-on-one selling scenario, a coach might ask, what's going on in your life that made you want to take this call? And when you think about it, it's the ultimate leaping off point and it really plays into our next principle, which is confidence. You know, when you're able to confidently and accurately reflect back your prospects context back to them, it instills confidence that, first of all, you get it. You get where they're coming from. You get what brought them here. You get their resistance. You get their skepticism. You get their hope. You get it all. And because of that, because you get it, because you get everything they are bringing to the table, the outcomes you're promising now feel possible because it's grounded within their current reality. It's not this lofty thing that feels unattainable. It's grounded in the context of where they're currently at. You know, so you root these aspirations in a clear and complete understanding of the starting point, and that is extremely powerful. The next thing is courage. You know, in the presence of, you know, these people that we're talking about, we feel this rush of new possibility. You know, think about, you know, these people in your life that we highlighted earlier in this lesson, you know, that rush of excitement you feel, you know, places where you had felt stuck or constricted before their influence and presence makes us now feel expansive, like it's safe to expand and go on on that journey. There's this renewed sense of possibility. You know, they give us the courage to commit to a more empowering vision, and it's very subtle yet very real. If you're really imagining this person in your life, whose presence makes you feel ready to expand, ready to grow in certain ways, that safety in it, that courage and stills, that's what we want to give to our audience. Once again, at scale, 60,000 people in a single afternoon. You know, and likewise, they have the courage to ask us the tough questions and not let us off the hook of that commitment. You know, they challenge us. Just like at the beginning, you know, they challenge our beliefs and views. They continue to challenge us deeply. You know, whenever we start to backslide into our beliefs or fall into old habits, patterns of resistance, a good friend, you know, a good coach, they will hold our feet to the fire and remind us of our commitment. You know, so as a conversion coach, your main focus should be simply not letting your customers fail. You know, if they've committed to an outcome in that high moment of courage, you know, they've committed, yes, I want to do this. Yes, I see that you've reflected back everything about my prior context. And now I believe that I could do this and I'm committed to doing this. Part of your responsibility is not letting them fail to challenge them, not just initially with beliefs in a new way, but challenge them throughout the funnel, especially towards the close to hold true to that commitment. Why? I mean, you know, a skeptic could say because we want more sales. But what we're really going for here is simply care because you care. You don't just pay lip service to it. Just like how you feel when someone legitimately cares about you, it has nothing to do with pushing a self-serving agenda, you know, which most people could sniff out a mile away. You know, they're not asking you to change into a version of you that would be more convenient to them. They're inviting you to change because they care deeply and intimately about your growth and helping you stay true to the commitments you made to yourself. And it's this authentic care that makes you feel so supported and courageous in their presence. It's that level of care that I want you to develop with your prospects and your funnels. You know, J. Abraham talks a lot about preeminence, you know, it means always having your audience's best interests in mind, you know, and David Ogilvy, like decades prior said the customer is not a moron. She's your wife. And further to that, you know, FOMO shouldn't just be this thing you ask your customer to feel in the minutes before the cart closes. FOMO should be something you feel on behalf of them. When you sense their opportunity to overcome a pain or solve a problem is slipping. You know, these are the qualities, you know, that I really want you to cultivate as marketers, as educators, as coaches and consultants. You know, that's really take these five C's and make them, you know, a part of every interaction, you know, especially just caring about them, caring about them. And that really is what earns you the right to hold them to those commitments. You know, not in any kind of self-serving way, not because you want the sale, but because, you know, you're just really furthering their advancement, their growth, their overcoming of trials and tribulations. And to me, that is one of the finest gifts we can give as marketers, as educators, as entrepreneurs. You know, that's what brought many of us in the game to begin with, to solve these problems, solve them at scale and get rewarded for it. So it's really just honoring the essence of what we're doing as entrepreneurs. And the final thing I want to talk about in this lesson is interruption versus permission versus invitation marketing. You know, many of us have heard the term interruption marketing, which is essentially an ad that interrupts your prospect. You know, and we've heard the term permission marketing, which is when the prospect raises their hand and says, I want to hear from you. So this will usually come in the form of an email opt-in. But I want to challenge you right now to drop the term permission marketing, because it's just far too timid for the level of work and the level of care. We're trying to express through our marketing. So for the level of the work that we have ahead of us, you know, where we're literally taking our prospect by the hand and coaching them into being the kind of person ready and empowered to get the most out of your product. You know, we really need to go one level deeper. You know, permission is the moment they opt in from there on out. It is invitation. You've been invited into their life and you have to assume until they say otherwise that they want you there, you know, until they state otherwise explicitly, you need to assume that not only are you permitted, but you're welcomed and required to act in their best interest. So in the next module, you'll get introduced to a concept I call state checks, you know, and this is pretty much just reasserting, you know, that invitation and a few critical junctures in your funnel so that you never have to feel bad or timid about, you know, being too challenging or emailing with too much frequency. You know, for now, all you really need to know is that they asked you to be there. You know, they flashed the SOS signal in the air. They flashed that bat signal. And now it's your responsibility to deliver on the value they're expecting from you. So you don't need to be apologetic about emailing them. You don't need to be apologetic about being confronting and challenging when the moment calls for it. Be like their most trusted friend who calls them out on their stuff, who gives an objective view of what's holding them back and the encouragement and tools to get through it. So that's it. That's what I want to leave you with here. And before I head off and meet you in module two, I'm just going to give you a brief breakdown on why I hired my first coach, you know, a couple of years ago. Um, it's been about two and a half, three years ago now that I hired my first coach. And it took me a while to unpack why I hired him. But looking back, you know, retrospectively, all these factors were present. You know, the decision was scary as hell. I was literally three days out of the stroke ward when I met him. I had quit my full-time job, had no income, had no idea how long it would take to feel normal again. You know, I remember my left hand was still typing faster than my right hand, which is kind of problematic for a writer. And by all fair estimations, I was in no place to invest in a coach. Yet in that single hour, he made me feel more heard and understood than anyone before. He convinced me that my vision mattered and he challenged me to boldly commit to it. So even though the price tag at the time was enough to freak me out, the courage he helped me find in that moment and the confidence that I could do it was all I needed to really sign on that dotted line on that day. So it wasn't predatory or sleazy or anything like that. It was preeminent and of the highest service. And the results of that experience and have been truly life changing. So once again, part of what we're doing in a long-term online marketing funnel is trying to gradually level up those 10x conversion rates that one-on-one collaborative selling can boast, but just doing it at scale. And step one of doing that is being aware of these five traits, you know, of a successful coach and consciously cultivating them, building them into your communications with your audience. The rest of the course will be all about how you can actually craft these elements into your sales messaging in a very natural and authentic way that actually makes writing feel like a joy and a gift. But with that, we're going to take a break. Then we're going to meet back in module two where you'll perhaps for the first time really, and I mean really, really meet your customer. So I'll see you there.
